2. `Common.props` adds `Microsoft.Extensions.Logging` Version 9.0.0 to all projects
3. Projects have PackageReferences with explicit versions and some using MSBuild properties

**Packages across all projects (with versions):**
- `Microsoft.Extensions.Logging` - 9.0.0 (from Common.props, all projects)


- `System.Text.Json` - varies by project (10.0.1 in Api, Tests, Web; 9.0.0 in Core; 8.0.5 in Legacy), so I'll use 10.0.1 as the highest
- `Azure.Storage.Blobs` - 12.24.0 from the property in Api, 12.19.0 in Legacy, so 12.24.0 is the max
- `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Mvc.NewtonsoftJson` - has conditional versions depending on the target framework (6.0.36 for net6.0, 8.0.11 for net8.0)
- `Microsoft.Extensions.Hosting` - 8.0.1 from Core
- `OpenTelemetry.Extensions.Hosting` - 1.15.0 from Web
- `xunit` - 2.9.3 from Tests

Now I'm thinking through how Central Package Management handles conditional references. With CPM, the version moves to Directory.Packages.props while the project files just reference the package without specifying a version. For packages with different versions across different target frameworks, like the Newtonsoft.Json package, I can keep the conditional PackageReference items in the project file while the version gets centrally managed.

Since the user wants me to use the highest version for conflicts, I'll go with 8.0.11 for Microsoft.AspNetCore.Mvc.NewtonsoftJson. Though these aren't really conflicts—they're intentional version differences per TFM—CPM requires a single version in Directory.Packages.props, so the highest version makes sense.

The conditional ItemGroups in Api.csproj based on TargetFramework will still work fine; they control whether the package is referenced at all, while the actual version comes from the central props file.

Now I'm setting up the Directory.Packages.props file with the centralized package versions...

Creating the props file with package version entries... Updating the project files to centralize package versions...
